title: Sonya Tonyokwe & Rozena Tonyokwe for herself and on behalf of Tybyn Samuel v. Erakdrik Samuel & Mudge Samuel,
text:
FILED
JUN 212023
IN THE SUPREME COURT
REPUBLIC OF THE MARSHALL ISLANDS
SONYA TONYOKWE & ROZENA TONYOKWE ) S.Ct. Case No.2022-00889
for herself and on behalf of TIBYN SAMUEL, )
Plaintiffs/A ppellees, )
) ORD.ER DISMISSING
vs. ) APPEAL
)
ERAKDRIK SAMUEL & MUDGE SAMUEL, )
_______De_fen_da_nts/_A p_pell_ant_s. ___ )
)
TO ALL PARTIES HEREIN THROUGH THEIR COUNSEL OF RECORD
BEFORE FULL PANEL OF THE SUPREME COURT: Cadra, C.J.; Seabright, A.J.1 and
;
Seeborg, A.J.2
I. INTRODUCTION
On or about June 12, 2023, (AST), a single-judge procedural order was entered upon an
unopposed motion filed by Appellees DISMISSING the above captioned appeal for Appellants'
failure to timely file their Opening Brief within the 40 days after service/filing of the Record on
Appeal allowed by Supreme Court Rule of Procedure (SCRP), Rule 28(b).
On or about June 13, 2023, Appellants filed and served on Appellees what is styled a
"[Motion] Requesting Temporary Suspension/Stay of Order Dismissing Appeal." Appellants
argue that the Order Dismissing Appeal should be "temporarily suspended or stayed" because
"Plaintiffs/A ppellants did not receive any notice or court stamped copies of the Motion to
Dismiss from the Clerk of Courts to comply with the SCRP Rule 42(b)(2), (3)."
1
Hon. J. Michael Seabright, District Judge, United States District Court for the District of Hawaii, sitting as
RMI Supreme Court Associate Justice by designation of the Cabinet.
2
Hon. Richard Seeborg, Chief Judge, United States District Court, Northern District of California, sitting as RMI
Supreme Court Associate Justice by designation of the Cabinet.
I

Appellees have filed no opposition or response to the instant motion.
The full panel of the Supreme Court liberally construes Appellants' instant motion as an
application for vacation or modification of the June 12, 2023, single judge Order Dismissing
Appeal pursuant to SCRP, Rule 27 (c).
Upon full-panel review, the Court AFFIRMS THE ORDER DISMISSING THIS
APPEAL for the reasons set forth below.
II. DISCUSSION
A. Appellants' Contentions.
As best as we can derive from Appellants' instant filing, Appellants are making two
arguments that the single-judge procedural order dismissing their appeal rshould be "temporarily
suspended/stayed" (in their words), or otherwise modified or vacated:
I. Appellants first argue that "Plaintiffs/A ppellants did not receive any notice and/or
Court stamped copies of the [Appellees'] Motion to Dismiss from the Clerk of Courts to comply
with the SCRP Rule 42(b)(2),(3)." See "[Motion] Requesting Temporary Suspension/Stay of
Order Dismissing Appeal," p. I.
2. Appellants then attempt to establish "good cause" for their failure to file their
Opening Brief within the 40 days allowed by SCRP, Rule 28(b) by claiming that Appellants'
counsel was not served by e-mail by the Clerk of Courts "regarding the Filing of Record on
Appeal or any Notice of Designation of Records, in (sic) pursuant to SCR[P] 28(b)." See
"Declaration of Counsel (Jack Jorbon)," p. 2, para. 5. The apparent theory being that the Clerk's
alleged failure to serve Notice of Filing the Record on Appeal did not commence the 40-day time
period for filing Appellants' Opening Brief. Therefore, filing of the Opening Brief should be
2

deemed timely because the 40-day period in which to file had not commenced.
B. Appellant's First Argument Fails Because The Clerk of the Supreme Court
Is Not Responsible for Making Service of Appellees' Motion to Dismiss on
Appellants.
Appellants' counsel states "I was under the impression that the Clerk of Courts would
have sent notice and enclosed court stamped copy(s) to me, as she usually does, via e-mail of the
Plaintiffs Motion to Dismiss the Appeal Case so that I could have filed an Opposition Motion
for the Defendants/A ppellants on time." See, "Declaration of Counsel (Jack Jo rbon)," pp. 1-2,
paragraph 4.
The Supreme Court Rules of Procedure, not counsel's impressions, govern service of
documents in appellate cases. The Clerk of the Supreme Court is not responsible for serving a
party's motions on other parties to the proceeding. In this case the clerk of the Supreme Court
was not responsible for serving Appellees' Motion to Dismiss on Appellants.
SCRP, Rule 25(b) provides:
Service of All Papers Required. Copies of all papers filed by any party and not required
by these rules to be served by the clerk of the Supreme Court shall, at or before or
promptly after the time of filing, be served by a party or person acting for a party on all
other parties to the proceeding. Service on a party represented by counsel shall be made
on counsel.
The rules do not require the clerk of the Supreme Court to serve the parties with motions
filed by another party to the appeal. Thus, Appellants' argument that the, clerk of the Supreme
Court somehow failed to make service of the Motion to Dismiss is without merit.
Under Rule 25(b) Appellees had the burden of serving Appellants' counsel with the
Motion to Dismiss. Review of the record indicates that Appellees complied with their duty by
serving Appellants' counsel, Jack Jorbon, with the Motion to Dismiss by e-mail at
3

jackjorbonl9@gmail.com. See "Affidavit of Service" accompanying Appellees' "Motion to
Dismiss" dated June 5, 2023. Significantly, Appellants' counsel does not deny receipt of
Appellees' e-mail serving the Motion to Dismiss.
C. Appellants' Second Argument Fails Because The Record Demonstrates the
Clerk of Court Served the "Notice of Filing the Record on Appeal" on
Appellants Which Was Received by Appellants' Counsel.
Appellants' counsel claims "I have searched my records and I could not find any e-mail
from the Clerk of Courts regarding the Filing of Record on Appeal or any Notice of Designation
of Records, in (sic) pursuant to SCR Rule 28(b)." Significantly, Counsel,does not claim he never
received the Notice of Filing of Record on Appeal but, rather, that he cannot find the e-mail.
Appellants' counsel's apparent assertion that he never received tlie Notice of Filing the
Record on Appeal is belied by the record. The record reveals:
1. That the Supreme Court Clerk filed the "Clerk's Certificate In Re: Filing Record
on Appeal" on February 17, 2023. See Exhibit A attached.
2. That Appellants' counsel Jack Jorbon, was served with the "Record on Appeal"n
February 18, 2023. See Exhibit B "Certificate of Service" attached.
3. That Appellants' counsel Jack Jorbon was served by e-mail at
jackjorbonl9@gmail.com on February 18, 2023.
4. That Appellants' counsel Jack Jorbon received the e-mail service of the "Record
on Appeal" as is evidenced by Jack Jorbon's e-mail to the Clerk ofCoul'ts dated February 22,
2023. See Exhibit C "e-mail exchange between Clerk of Courts and Jack Jorbon" attached.
5. SCRP, Rule 25 ( c) provides "service by e-mail is complete upon the transmission
being received and must be evidenced by the transmittal e-mail and a receipt e-mail." Exhibit C
4

evidences service on Appellants of the Notice of Filing of Record on ApJjeal.
6. The 40-day period for filing Appellant's Opening Brief commenced on the date of
service, February 18, 2023, pursuant to SCRP, Rule 28(b).
7. Appellants late filed their Opening Brief on April 18, 2023 ( 19 days past the 40-
day deadline imposed by SCRP, Rule 28(b)).
We find and conclude Appellants were properly served with Appellees' Motion to
Dismiss and failed to file an opposition or response within the five days permitted by SCRP,
Rule 27(a).
We also find and conclude that Appellants' counsel was properly,served with the Clerk's
Notice of Filing the Record on Appeal on February 18, 2023, which commenced the 40-day
period for filing the Opening Brief. Appellants did not timely file the Opening Brief and have not
demonstrated "good cause" for their failure to timely file. We therefore AFFIRM the single
judge procedural order dated June 12, 2023, dismissing this appeal and ORDER the appeal
DISMISSED.
Dated: June 21, 2023
5
